\begin{array} { r l } & { \cfrac { 1 } { \Delta } = \left[ r ^ { 2 } + r ^ { \prime 2 } - 2 r r ^ { \prime } \left( \cos ( \theta - \theta ^ { \prime } ) + \Psi \right) \right] ^ { - 1 / 2 } } \\ & { \quad = \cfrac { 1 } { \Delta _ { 0 } } + r r ^ { \prime } \Psi \cdot \cfrac { 1 } { \Delta _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } } + \cfrac { 3 } { 2 } \left( r r ^ { \prime } \Psi \right) ^ { 2 } \cdot \cfrac { 1 } { \Delta _ { 0 } ^ { 5 } } + \cdots } \\ & { \quad = \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 0 } ^ { \infty } \cfrac { ( 2 i ) ! } { ( i ! ) ^ { 2 } } \cdot \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } r r ^ { \prime } \Psi \right) ^ { i } \cdot \cfrac { 1 } { \Delta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 i + 1 } } , } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\frac { 1 } { \Delta _ { 0 } } = \left[ r ^ { 2 } + r ^ { \prime 2 } - 2 r r ^ { \prime } \cos ( \theta - \theta ^ { \prime } ) \right] ^ { - 1 / 2 } .
$$

Let

$$
\rho _ { 0 } = \left[ a ^ { 2 } + a ^ { \prime 2 } - 2 a a ^ { \prime } \cos ( \theta - \theta ^ { \prime } ) \right] ^ { 1 / 2 } .
$$

Using a Taylor series expansion in $\rho _ { 0 }$ , we can write

$$
\frac { 1 } { \Delta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 i + 1 } } = \frac { 1 } { \rho _ { 0 } ^ { 2 i + 1 } } + ( r - a ) \frac { \partial } { \partial a } \left( \frac { 1 } { \rho _ { 0 } ^ { 2 i + 1 } } \right) + ( r ^ { \prime } - a ^ { \prime } ) \frac { \partial } { \partial a ^ { \prime } } \left( \frac { 1 } { \rho _ { 0 } ^ { 2 i + 1 } } \right) + \cdots .
$$

Let $D _ { m , n }$ denote the differential operator

$$
D _ { m , n } = a ^ { m } a ^ { \prime } { } ^ { n } \frac { \partial ^ { m + n } } { \partial a ^ { m } \partial a ^ { \prime } { } ^ { n } } ,
$$

and let

$$
\varepsilon = \frac { r } { a } - 1 , \quad \quad \varepsilon ^ { \prime } = \frac { r ^ { \prime } } { a ^ { \prime } } - 1 .
$$

From the expansion of $r / a$ given in Eq. (2.81), it is clear that $\varepsilon$ is of $\mathcal { O } ( e )$ and $\varepsilon ^ { \prime }$ is of $\mathcal { O } ( e ^ { \prime } )$ . Hence we have

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { \Delta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 i + 1 } } = \left[ 1 + \varepsilon D _ { 1 , 0 } + \varepsilon ^ { \prime } D _ { 0 , 1 } + \right. } } \\ { { \displaystyle \left. \frac { 1 } { 2 ! } \left( \varepsilon ^ { 2 } D _ { 2 , 0 } + 2 \varepsilon \varepsilon ^ { \prime } D _ { 1 , 1 } + \varepsilon ^ { \prime 2 } D _ { 0 , 2 } \right) + \cdot \cdot \cdot \right] \frac { 1 } { \rho _ { 0 } ^ { 2 i + 1 } } . } } \end{array}
$$

However, from Eq. (6.57),

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \frac { 1 } { \rho _ { 0 } ^ { 2 i + 1 } } } = \left[ a ^ { 2 } + a ^ { \prime 2 } - 2 a a ^ { \prime } \cos ( \theta - \theta ^ { \prime } ) \right] ^ { - \left( i + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) } } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \quad = a ^ { \prime - ( 2 i + 1 ) } \left[ 1 + \alpha ^ { 2 } - 2 \alpha \cos ( \theta - \theta ^ { \prime } ) \right] ^ { - \left( i + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) } } } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \quad = a ^ { \prime - ( 2 i + 1 ) } \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { j = - \infty } ^ { \infty } b _ { i + \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ^ { ( j ) } ( \alpha ) \cos j ( \theta - \theta ^ { \prime } ) } , } } \end{array}
$$

where the $b _ { s } ^ { ( j ) } ( \alpha )$ are Laplace coefficients, each of which can be expressed as a uniformly convergent series in $\alpha$ for all $\alpha < 1$ . Since the $D _ { m , n }$ operators act only on the Laplace coefficients, we can define functions $A _ { i , j , m , n }$ by

$$
A _ { i , j , m , n } = D _ { m , n } \left( a ^ { \prime - ( 2 i + 1 ) } b _ { i + \frac 1 2 } ^ { ( j ) } ( \alpha ) \right) = a ^ { m } a ^ { \prime n } \frac { \partial ^ { m + n } } { \partial a ^ { m } \partial a ^ { \prime n } } \left( a ^ { \prime - ( 2 i + 1 ) } b _ { i + \frac 1 2 } ^ { ( j ) } ( \alpha ) \right) ,
$$

and we can now write

$$
\frac { 1 } { \Delta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 i + 1 } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { j = - \infty } ^ { \infty } \left[ A _ { i , j , 0 , 0 } + \varepsilon A _ { i , j , 1 , 0 } + \varepsilon ^ { \prime } A _ { i , j , 0 , 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \right] \cos j ( \theta - \theta ^ { \prime } ) .
$$

If we generalise this expression we obtain

$$
\frac { 1 } { \Delta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 i + 1 } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { j = - \infty } ^ { \infty } \left[ \sum _ { l = 0 } ^ { \infty } \frac { 1 } { l ! } \sum _ { k = 0 } ^ { l } \left( \begin{array} { l } { { l } } \\ { { k } } \end{array} \right) \varepsilon ^ { k } \varepsilon ^ { \prime l - k } A _ { i , j , k , l - k } \right] \cos j ( \theta - \theta ^ { \prime } ) .
$$

Care must be taken in the calculation of the partial derivatives with respect to $a$ and $a ^ { \prime }$ in the $A _ { i , j , k , l - k }$ since $a$ and $a ^ { \prime }$ are still contained implicitly within the Laplace coefficients b(j) 1 $b _ { i + \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ^ { ( j ) } ( a / a ^ { \prime } )$ .

Substituting Eq. (6.65) in Eq. (6.55) we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { D } } = \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 0 } ^ { \infty } \frac { ( 2 i ) ! } { ( i ! ) ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { r } { a } \frac { r ^ { \prime } } { a ^ { \prime } } \Psi \right) ^ { i } \frac { a ^ { i } a ^ { \prime } i ^ { + 1 } } { 2 } } \\ & { \qquad \times \displaystyle \sum _ { j = - \infty } ^ { \infty } \left[ \sum _ { l = 0 } ^ { \infty } \frac { 1 } { l ! } \sum _ { k = 0 } ^ { l } \binom { l } { k } \varepsilon ^ { k } \varepsilon ^ { \prime l - k } A _ { i , j , k , l - k } \right] \cos j ( \theta - \theta ^ { \prime } ) . } \end{array}
$$

It is worthwhile noting that the inclinations, $I$ and $I ^ { \prime }$ , are only contained in $\Psi$ and the eccentricities are only contained in the $\varepsilon$ and $\varepsilon ^ { \prime }$ terms in Eq. (6.66).

The expansion of the indirect parts, $\mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { E } }$ and $\mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { I } }$ , is more straightforward using the series obtained from expanding $\cos \psi$ in Eq. (6.50) and the series given in Sect. 2.5. Note that the expansion of these terms does not involve Laplace coefficients.

The literal expansion makes use of Laplace coefficients, which are explicit functions of $\alpha$ rather than the individual coefficients of powers of $\alpha$ that we encountered in Kaula’s expansion. The Laplace coefficient b(j) 1 $b _ { i + \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ^ { ( j ) } ( \alpha )$ in Eq. (6.62) is defined by

$$
\frac { 1 } { 2 } b _ { s } ^ { ( j ) } ( \alpha ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \frac { \cos j \psi { \mathrm { d } } \psi } { ( 1 - 2 \alpha \cos \psi + \alpha ^ { 2 } ) ^ { s } } ,
$$

where $s = i + 1 / 2$ is a half-integer (i.e., $s = 1 / 2 , 3 / 2 , 5 / 2 , . . . )$ and $\alpha = a / a ^ { \prime }$ . Alternatively we can write this in series form as

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac 1 2 b _ { s } ^ { ( j ) } ( \alpha ) = \frac { s ( s + 1 ) \ldots ( s + j - 1 ) } { 1 \cdot 2 \cdot 3 \cdot \ldots j } \alpha ^ { j } } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad \times \left[ 1 + \frac { s ( s + j ) } { 1 ( j + 1 ) } \alpha ^ { 2 } + \frac { s ( s + 1 ) ( s + j ) ( s + j + 1 ) } { 1 \cdot 2 ( j + 1 ) ( j + 2 ) } \alpha ^ { 4 } + \ldots \right] . } \end{array}
$$

In the case where $j = 0$ the factor outside the brackets is equal to unity. It can be shown that the series definition of the Laplace coefficient is always convergent for $\alpha < 1$ .

Useful relations between Laplace coefficients and their derivatives are given in Brouwer & Clemence (1961). These include

$$
b _ { s } ^ { ( - j ) } = b _ { s } ^ { ( j ) } ,
$$

$$
D b _ { s } ^ { ( j ) } = s \left( b _ { s + 1 } ^ { ( j - 1 ) } - 2 \alpha b _ { s + 1 } ^ { ( j ) } + b _ { s + 1 } ^ { ( j + 1 ) } \right) ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { D ^ { n } b _ { s } ^ { ( j ) } = s \Big ( D ^ { n - 1 } b _ { s + 1 } ^ { ( j - 1 ) } - 2 \alpha D ^ { n - 1 } b _ { s + 1 } ^ { ( j ) } } \\ & { \qquad + D ^ { n - 1 } b _ { s + 1 } ^ { ( j + 1 ) } - 2 ( n - 1 ) D ^ { n - 2 } b _ { s + 1 } ^ { ( j ) } \Big ) , } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \alpha ^ { n } \left( D ^ { n } b _ { s } ^ { ( j ) } - D ^ { n } b _ { s } ^ { ( j - 2 ) } \right) = } \\ & { \qquad - ( j + n - 1 ) \alpha ^ { n - 1 } D ^ { n - 1 } b _ { s } ^ { ( j ) } - ( j - n - 1 ) \alpha ^ { n - 1 } D ^ { n - 1 } b _ { s } ^ { ( j - 2 ) } } \\ & { \qquad + 2 ( j - 1 ) \left[ \alpha ^ { n } D ^ { n - 1 } b _ { s } ^ { ( j - 1 ) } + ( n - 1 ) \alpha ^ { n - 1 } D ^ { n - 2 } b _ { s } ^ { ( j - 1 ) } \right] , } \end{array}
$$

where $n \geq 2$ in the last two relations and $D \equiv \mathrm { d } / \mathrm { d } \alpha$ is a differential operator.

# 6.5 Literal Expansion to Second Order

As an illustration of the techniques outlined in Sect. 6.4, we will now derive an expansion of the disturbing function complete to second order in the eccentricities and inclinations.

To derive a series expansion for cos $\psi$ we first need to make use of the expansions for sin $f$ and cos $f$ in terms of the mean anomaly $M$ , given in Eqs. (2.84) and (2.85) respectively. To second order we have

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \sin f = \sin M + { { e } \sin 2 M } + { { e } ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { 9 } { 8 } \sin 3 M - \frac { 7 } { 8 } \sin M \right) , } \\ { \displaystyle \cos f = \cos M + { { e } \cos 2 M } - 1 ) + { { e } ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { 9 } { 8 } \cos 3 M - \frac { 9 } { 8 } \cos M \right) . } \end{array}
$$

Hence

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l } & { \cos [ \omega + f ] = \cos \omega \cos f - \sin \omega \sin f } \\ & { \qquad \approx \cos [ \omega + M ] + e \left( \cos [ \omega + 2 M ] - \cos \omega \right) } \\ & { \qquad + e ^ { 2 } \left( - \cos [ \omega + M ] - { \frac { 1 } { 8 } } \cos [ \omega - M ] + { \frac { 9 } { 8 } } \cos [ \omega + 3 M ] \right) } \end{array} }
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \sin [ \omega + f ] = \sin \omega \cos f + \cos \omega \sin f } \\ { \displaystyle \approx \sin [ \omega + M ] + e \left( \sin [ \omega + 2 M ] - \sin \omega \right) } \\ { \displaystyle \quad + e ^ { 2 } \left( - \sin [ \omega + M ] + \frac { 1 } { 8 } \sin [ \omega - M ] + \frac { 9 } { 8 } \sin [ \omega + 3 M ] \right) . } \end{array}
$$

In keeping with a number of previous expansions (including that by Kaula discussed in Sect. 6.3) we wish to express the disturbing function in terms of powers of $\sin { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } I$ and $\sin { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } I ^ { \prime }$ rather than sines and cosines of the inclinations. Therefore we make use of the relations

$$
\cos I = 1 - 2 \sin ^ { 2 } { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } I = 1 - 2 s ^ { 2 }
$$

and

$$
\sin I = 2 \sin \frac { 1 } { 2 } I \left( 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { 1 } { 2 } I \right) ^ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } = 2 s + \mathcal { O } ( s ^ { 3 } ) ,
$$

where $s = \sin { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } I$ . Substitution of these expressions and our expansions of $\cos [ \omega + f ]$ and sin $[ \omega + f ]$ in Eqs. (6.51)–(6.53) gives

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \frac { x } { r } \approx \cos [ \omega + \Omega + M ] + e ( \cos [ \omega + \Omega + 2 M ] - \cos [ \omega + \Omega ] ) } } \\ & { } & { \qquad + e ^ { 2 } ( \frac 9 8 \cos [ \omega + \Omega + 3 M ] - \frac 1 8 \cos [ \omega + \Omega - M ] - \cos [ \omega + \Omega + M ] ) } \\ & { } & { \qquad + s ^ { 2 } ( \cos [ \omega - \Omega + M ] - \cos [ \omega + \Omega + M ] ) , \phantom { \omega \Omega ( \omega + \Omega + 2 M ] - \cos [ \omega + \Omega + M ] } ( 6 . } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \frac { y } { r } \approx \sin [ \omega + \Omega + M ] + e ( \sin [ \omega + \Omega + 2 M ] - \sin [ \omega + \Omega ] ) } } \\ & { } & { \quad + e ^ { 2 } ( \frac 9 8 \sin [ \omega + \Omega + 3 M ] - \frac 1 8 \sin [ \omega + \Omega - M ] - \sin [ \omega + \Omega + M ] ) } \\ & { } & { \quad - s ^ { 2 } ( \sin [ \omega - \Omega + M ] + \sin [ \omega + \Omega + M ] ) , \qquad ( 6 } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
{ \frac { z } { r } } \approx 2 s \sin [ \omega + M ] + 2 e s \left( \sin [ \omega + 2 M ] - \sin \omega \right) .
$$

Similar expressions can be obtained for $x ^ { \prime } / r ^ { \prime } , y ^ { \prime } / r ^ { \prime }$ , and $z ^ { \prime } / r ^ { \prime }$ by replacing unprimed quantities by primed quantities in the above equations. Hence we can derive an expression for cos $\psi$ using Eq. (6.50). At the same time we can use the relations $M = \lambda - \varpi$ and $\omega = \varpi - \Omega$ to express the expansion in terms of longitudes. We get

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \cos \psi \approx } \\ & { ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } - s ^ { 2 } - s ^ { 2 } ) \cos [ \lambda - \lambda ^ { \prime } ] + e ^ { \epsilon } \cos [ 2 \lambda - 2 \lambda ^ { \prime } - \varpi + \varpi ^ { \prime } ] } \\ & { \quad + e ^ { \epsilon } \cos [ \varpi - \varpi ^ { \prime } ] + 2 s ^ { \epsilon } \cos [ \lambda - \lambda ^ { \prime } - \Omega + \Omega ^ { \prime } ] } \\ & { \quad + \cos [ 2 \lambda - \lambda ^ { \prime } - \varpi ] - \epsilon \cos [ \lambda ^ { \prime } - \varpi ] } \\ & { \quad + e ^ { \epsilon } \cos [ \lambda - 2 \lambda ^ { \prime } + \varpi ^ { \prime } ] - e ^ { \epsilon } \cos [ \lambda - \varpi ^ { \prime } ] } \\ & { \quad + \frac { \epsilon } { 9 } e ^ { 2 } \cos [ 3 \lambda - \lambda ^ { \prime } - 2 \varpi ] - \frac { 1 } { 8 } e ^ { 2 } \cos [ \lambda + \lambda ^ { \prime } - 2 \varpi ] } \\ & { \quad + \frac { 9 } { 8 } e ^ { 2 } \cos [ 3 \lambda - 3 \lambda ^ { \prime } + 2 \varpi ] - \frac { 1 } { 8 } e ^ { 2 } \cos [ 3 \lambda + \lambda ^ { \prime } - 2 \varpi ] } \\ & { \quad - e ^ { \epsilon } \cos [ 2 \lambda - \varpi - \varpi ^ { \prime } ] - e ^ { \epsilon } \cos [ 2 \lambda ^ { \prime } - \varpi - \varpi ^ { \prime } ] } \\ & { \quad + s ^ { 2 } \cos [ 3 \lambda + \lambda ^ { \prime } - 2 \Omega ] + s ^ { 2 } \cos [ \lambda + \lambda ^ { \prime } - 2 \Omega ^ { \prime } ] } \\ & { \quad - 2 s ^ { \epsilon } \cos [ \lambda + \lambda ^ { \prime } - \Omega - \Omega ^ { \prime } ] . } \end{array}
$$

Since $\theta = \omega + \Omega + f$ we have

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \cos [ \theta - \theta ^ { \prime } ] = ( \cos \Omega \cos [ \omega + f ] - \sin \Omega \sin [ \omega + f ] ) } \\ & { \qquad \times ( \cos \Omega ^ { \prime } \cos [ \omega ^ { \prime } + f ^ { \prime } ] - \sin \Omega ^ { \prime } \sin [ \omega ^ { \prime } + f ^ { \prime } ] ) } \\ & { \qquad + ( \sin \Omega \cos [ \omega + f ] + \cos \Omega \sin [ \omega + f ] ) } \\ & { \qquad \times ( \sin \Omega ^ { \prime } \cos [ \omega ^ { \prime } + f ^ { \prime } ] + \cos \Omega ^ { \prime } \sin [ \omega ^ { \prime } + f ^ { \prime } ] ) . } \end{array}
$$

By comparing this with Eqs. (6.51)–(6.53) we see that the expansion for $\cos [ \theta - \theta ^ { \prime } ]$ can be obtained from the expansion for cos $\psi$ by setting $I = I ^ { \prime } = 0$ . Since $\Psi = \cos { \psi } - \cos [ \theta - \theta ^ { \prime } ]$ , the expansion of cos $\psi$ shows that $\Psi$ is the inclination-dependent part of cos $\psi$ and

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \Psi = s ^ { 2 } \left( \cos [ \lambda + \lambda ^ { \prime } - 2 \Omega ] - \cos [ \lambda - \lambda ^ { \prime } ] \right) } } \\ { { \qquad + 2 s s ^ { \prime } \left( \cos [ \lambda - \lambda ^ { \prime } - \Omega + \Omega ^ { \prime } ] - \cos [ \lambda + \lambda ^ { \prime } - \Omega - \Omega ^ { \prime } ] \right) } } \\ { { \qquad + s ^ { \prime 2 } \left( \cos [ \lambda + \lambda ^ { \prime } - 2 \Omega ^ { \prime } ] - \cos [ \lambda - \lambda ^ { \prime } ] \right) . } } \end{array}
$$

Note that $\Psi$ is of second order in the inclinations.

Since $r / a = 1 + \mathcal { O } ( e )$ and $r ^ { \prime } / a ^ { \prime } = 1 + \mathcal { O } ( e ^ { \prime } )$ , it is clear that, to second order in the eccentricities and inclinations, we can write

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { r } { a } \frac { r ^ { \prime } } { a ^ { \prime } } \Psi \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } s ^ { 2 } \left( \cos [ \lambda + \lambda ^ { \prime } - 2 \Omega ] - \cos [ \lambda - \lambda ^ { \prime } ] \right) } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + s s ^ { \prime } \left( \cos [ \lambda - \lambda ^ { \prime } - \Omega + \Omega ^ { \prime } ] - \cos [ \lambda + \lambda ^ { \prime } - \Omega - \Omega ^ { \prime } ] \right) } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + \frac { 1 } { 2 } s ^ { \prime 2 } \left( \cos [ \lambda + \lambda ^ { \prime } - 2 \Omega ^ { \prime } ] - \cos [ \lambda - \lambda ^ { \prime } ] \right) , } } \end{array}
$$

which is independent of $e$ to this order. Since we are only interested in a secondorder expansion, and since $\Psi$ is already of second order, we can ignore second and higher powers of $\Psi$ .

We have now obtained the first of the two major terms required for the series for $\mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { D } }$ (see Eq. (6.66)). We need to derive an expression for cos $j [ \theta - \theta ^ { \prime } ]$ , where $j$ is an arbitrary integer. We start by noting that

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \cos j [ \theta - \theta ^ { \prime } ] = \cos j [ \omega + \Omega + f ] \cos j [ \omega ^ { \prime } + \Omega ^ { \prime } + f ^ { \prime } ] } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { + \sin j [ \omega + \Omega + f ] \sin j [ \omega ^ { \prime } + \Omega ^ { \prime } + f ^ { \prime } ] . } } \end{array}
$$

From Eq. (2.88) we have

$$
f = M + 2 e \sin M + \frac { 5 } { 4 } e ^ { 2 } \sin 2 M + \mathcal { O } ( e ^ { 3 } ) .
$$

If we substitute this expression in cos $j [ \omega + \Omega + f ]$ and sin $j [ \omega + \Omega + f ]$ , transform to longitudes as before, and carry out a Taylor series expansion we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \cos j \theta \approx ( 1 - j ^ { 2 } e ^ { 2 } ) \cos [ j \lambda ] } } \\ { { \qquad + \left( \frac 1 2 j ^ { 2 } e ^ { 2 } - \frac 5 8 j e ^ { 2 } \right) \cos [ ( 2 - j ) \lambda - 2 \varpi ] } } \\ { { \qquad + \left( \frac 1 2 j ^ { 2 } e ^ { 2 } + \frac 5 8 j e ^ { 2 } \right) \cos [ ( 2 + j ) \lambda - 2 \varpi ] } } \\ { { \qquad - j e \cos [ ( 1 - j ) \lambda - \varpi ] + j e \cos [ ( 1 + j ) \lambda - \varpi ] } } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \sin j \theta \approx ( 1 - j ^ { 2 } e ^ { 2 } ) \sin [ j \lambda ] } } \\ { { \ \qquad + \left( \frac 5 8 j e ^ { 2 } - \frac 1 2 j ^ { 2 } e ^ { 2 } \right) \sin [ ( 2 - j ) \lambda - 2 \varpi ] } } \\ { { \ \qquad + \left( \frac 5 8 j e ^ { 2 } + \frac 1 2 j ^ { 2 } e ^ { 2 } \right) \sin [ ( 2 + j ) \lambda - 2 \varpi ] } } \\ { { \ \qquad + j e \sin [ ( 1 - j ) \lambda - \varpi ] + j e \sin [ ( 1 + j ) \lambda - \varpi ] . } } \end{array}
$$

By substituting unprimed quantities for primed ones we can easily obtain similar expressions for cos $j \theta ^ { \prime }$ and sin $j \theta ^ { \prime }$ . The resulting expression for cos $j [ \theta - \theta ^ { \prime } ]$ is

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \mathrm { c s c } ( | t | - c | \mathcal { S } ) } \\ & { \mathrm { ( I - } i ^ { 2 } e ^ { - 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { 2 } c ^ { 2 } ) \cos [ i ( 0 - i ^ { 2 } ) ] } \\ & { + ( \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 6 } } e ^ { - 4 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { 2 } c ^ { 2 } ) \cos [ i ( 0 - i ^ { 2 } ) ] } \\ & { + ( \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { 2 } e ^ { - 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 5 } e ^ { 2 } ) \cos [ ( 2 - i ) ] + \sin [ j ^ { 2 } - 2 i ^ { 2 } ] } \\ & { + ( \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { - 6 } ) ( 1 + i ^ { 2 } ) \sin [ \sin [ \sin [ \sin [ \sin [ \sin [ \sin ] ] ] ] {  } } \\ & {  + i c \cos [ ( 1 + i ) ] \wedge - i ^ { 3 } e ^ { - 6 } ) } \\ & { + ( \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { - 6 } ) e ^ { - 6 } \frac { 1 } { 3 } e ^ { 2 } ) \cos [ i ] + ( 2 - j i ^ { 2 } ) \cos [ 1 } \\ & { + ( \frac { 1 } { 8 } e ^ { - 6 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { 2 } ) \cos [ i ( 0 - i ) ] + \sin [ j ^ { 2 } - 2 i ^ { 2 } ] } \\ & { - i ^ { 6 } \cos [ j ] + ( 1 - j ) \sin [ \cos [ 1 + ( 1 - j ) ] \cos [ 1 } \\ & { - j ^ { 2 } e ^ { - 6 } ] \cos [ ( 1 + i ) ] ) } \\ & { - j e ^ { - 6 } \cos [ \sin [ ( 1 - j ) ] + \sin [ \sin [ \sin [ \sin [ \sin [ \sin [ \sin ] ] ] ] {  } } \\ & {  + j e ^ { - 6 } ) \cos [ ( 1 - j ) ] } \\ & { + ( \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { - 6 } ) \cos [ ( 1 + i ^ { 2 } ) \sin [ \sin [ \sin [ \sin [ \sin ] ] ] {  } } \\ & {  + j e ^ { - 6 } ) \cos [ ( 1 - j ) ] } \\ & { + ( \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { - 1 } ) \cos [ ( 1 - j ) ] } \\ &  + ( \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { - 1 }  \end{array}
$$

Although the summation over $j$ in Eq. (6.66) is over all values, in practice we do not need to carry out this summation (see Sect. 6.9 for an example).

From Eqs. (6.60) and (2.81) we have

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \varepsilon = \displaystyle \frac { r } { a } - 1 \approx - e \cos M + \frac 1 2 e ^ { 2 } ( 1 - \cos 2 M ) } } \\ { { = - e \cos [ \lambda - \varpi ] + \frac 1 2 e ^ { 2 } \left( 1 - \cos [ 2 \lambda - 2 \varpi ] \right) } } \end{array}
$$

and hence

$$
\varepsilon ^ { 2 } \approx { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } e ^ { 2 } + { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } e ^ { 2 } \cos 2 M = { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } e ^ { 2 } + { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } e ^ { 2 } \cos [ 2 \lambda - 2 \varpi ] ,
$$

with similar expressions for $\varepsilon ^ { \prime }$ and $\varepsilon ^ { \prime 2 }$ . No powers beyond the second are necessary for this expansion since $\varepsilon$ is of $\mathcal { O } ( e )$ .

Finally, before carrying out the summation in Eq. (6.66), we need to calculate the derivatives of the Laplace coefficients given by the $A _ { i , j , m , n }$ function. This task can be simplified by noting that, for a given value of $i$ in the summation, we need to calculate

$$
a ^ { i } a ^ { \prime { i + 1 } } A _ { i , j , m , n } = a ^ { i + m } a ^ { \prime { i + n + 1 } } { \frac { \partial ^ { m + n } } { \partial a ^ { m } \partial a ^ { \prime { m } } } } \left( a ^ { \prime - ( 2 i + 1 ) } b _ { i + \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ^ { ( j ) } ( a / a ^ { \prime } ) \right) .
$$

The result of the differentiation is to leave a function of $a / a ^ { \prime }$ alone. In our case the required values of $A _ { i , j , m , n }$ are:

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { a ^ { i } a ^ { i + 1 } A _ { i , j , 0 , 0 } = \alpha ^ { i } b _ { i + \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ^ { ( j ) } ( \alpha ) , } \\ & { a ^ { i } a ^ { i + 1 } A _ { i , j , 1 , 0 } = \alpha ^ { i + 1 } D b _ { i + \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ^ { ( j ) } ( \alpha ) , } \\ & { a ^ { i } a ^ { i + 1 } A _ { i , j , 0 , 1 } = - \alpha ^ { i + 1 } D b _ { i + \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ^ { ( j ) } ( \alpha ) - ( 2 i + 1 ) \alpha ^ { i } b _ { i + \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ^ { ( j ) } ( \alpha ) , } \\ & { a ^ { i } a ^ { i + 1 } A _ { i , j , 2 , 0 } = \alpha ^ { i + 2 } D ^ { 2 } b _ { i + \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ^ { ( j ) } ( \alpha ) , } \\ & { a ^ { i } a ^ { i + 1 } A _ { i , j , 1 , 1 } = - \alpha ^ { i + 2 } D ^ { 2 } b _ { i + \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ^ { ( j ) } ( \alpha ) - 2 \alpha ^ { i + 1 } ( i + 1 ) D b _ { i + \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ^ { ( j ) } ( \alpha ) , } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { r c l } { { } } & { { } } & { { a ^ { i } a ^ { \prime } { } ^ { i + 1 } A _ { i , j , 2 , 2 } = \alpha ^ { i + 2 } D ^ { 2 } b _ { i + \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ^ { ( j ) } ( \alpha ) + 4 \alpha ^ { i + 1 } ( i + 1 ) D b _ { i + \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ^ { ( j ) } ( \alpha ) } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { { } } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { + 2 \alpha ^ { i } ( 2 i ^ { 2 } + 3 i + 2 ) b _ { i + \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ^ { ( j ) } ( \alpha ) , } } \end{array}
$$

where $i$ will take the values 0 and 1; higher values can be ignored because of the presence of the $\Psi ^ { i }$ term in Eq. (6.66).

We are now in a position to carry out the summation over $i$ . To second order in the eccentricity and inclination we have

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { D } } = ( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \big [ a ^ { \prime } A _ { 0 , j , 0 , 0 } + \varepsilon a ^ { \prime } A _ { 0 , j , 1 , 0 } + \varepsilon ^ { \prime } a ^ { \prime } A _ { 0 , j , 0 , 1 }  } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad + \varepsilon ^ { 2 } a ^ { \prime } A _ { 0 , j , 2 , 0 } + \varepsilon \varepsilon ^ { \prime } a ^ { \prime } A _ { 0 , j , 1 , 1 } + \varepsilon ^ { \prime 2 } a ^ { \prime } A _ { 0 , j , 0 , 2 } \big ] } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad + ( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { r } { a } \frac { r ^ { \prime } } { a ^ { \prime } } \Psi ) a a ^ { \prime 2 } A _ { 1 , j , 0 , 0 } ) \cos j [ \theta - \theta ^ { \prime } ] . } \end{array}
$$

Using the series that we have already derived for the quantities in this equation, we get an expansion with twenty-three cosine arguments. These can be categorised by the order of the argument, which is simply the sum of the coefficients of $\lambda$ and $\lambda ^ { \prime }$ . If we write the second-order expansion as

$$
\mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { D } } = \mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { D } } ^ { ( 0 ) } + \mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { D } } ^ { ( 1 ) } + \mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { D } } ^ { ( 2 ) } ,
$$

where $\mathcal { R } ^ { ( i ) }$ denotes the part of the expansion containing the arguments of order

$i$ , then

$$

\left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sqrt { \pi ^ { 5 } } \left( 2 \sigma ^ { 2 } - \sigma \right) \sigma _ { 0 } ^ { ( 2 ) } \right) \sigma _ { 0 } ( 1 - \ j + \ j + \cdots , j - \cdots ) } \\ & { \quad - \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sqrt { \pi ^ { 5 } } \left( 1 \sigma ^ { 2 } - \sigma \big ( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sqrt { \pi ^ { 5 } } \big ( 1 + j \big ) ^ { 2 } - 2 \sigma \big ( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sqrt { \pi ^ { 5 } } \big ( 1 - j \big ) ^ { 2 } - 2 \sigma \ \end{array}

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \quad + \left( \frac { 1 } { 1 6 } e ^ { 2 } \left[ ( 4 - 9 j ) + 4 j ^ { 2 } + 6 6 { \cal O } - 4 j \omega { \cal O } + \omega ^ { 2 } { \cal D } ^ { 2 } \right] \delta _ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } ^ { ( 3 ) } \right) } \\ & { \quad \times \cos [ \beta ( \lambda ) + ( 2 - j ) \lambda ^ { 2 } - 2 \omega ^ { 2 } ] } \\ & { \quad + \left( \frac { 1 } { 4 } s ^ { 2 } [ \omega ] \delta _ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } ^ { ( 4 ) } \right) \cos [ ( 1 - j ) \lambda + ( 1 + j ) \lambda ^ { 4 } - 2 \Omega ] } \\ & { \quad - \left( \frac { 1 } { 4 } s ^ { 2 } [ \omega ] \delta _ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } ^ { ( 6 ) } \right) \cos [ ( 1 + j ) \lambda + ( 1 - j ) \lambda ^ { 4 } - 2 \Omega ] } \\ & { \quad + \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } s ^ { 2 } [ - \alpha ] b _ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } ^ { ( 2 ) } \right) \cos [ ( 1 - j ) \lambda + ( 1 + j ) \lambda ^ { 2 } - \Omega - 2 \Omega ] } \\ & { \quad - \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } s ^ { 2 } [ - \alpha ] b _ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } ^ { ( 2 ) } \right) \cos [ ( 1 + j ) \lambda + ( 1 - j ) \lambda ^ { 2 } - 8 - 2 \Omega ] } \\ & { \quad + \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } s ^ { 2 } [ - \alpha ] b _ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } ^ { ( 2 ) } \right) \cos [ ( 1 - j ) \lambda + ( 1 - j ) \lambda ^ { 2 } - 2 \Omega ] } \\ & { \quad + \left( \frac { 1 } { 4 } s ^ { 2 } [ \omega ] b _ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } ^ { ( 6 ) } \right) \cos [ ( 1 - j ) \lambda + ( 1 - j ) \lambda ^ { 2 } - 2 \Omega ] } \\ & { \quad + \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } s ^ { 2 } [ \omega ] b _ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } ^ { ( 6 ) } \right) \cos [ \beta ( \lambda ) + ( 1 - j ) \lambda ^ { 2 } - 2 \Omega ] . } \end{array}
$$

The arguments in this expansion are not all unique and further simplification is possible. This is clear from an inspection of the different terms since, apart from the first term in $\mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { D } } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ , they occur in pairs with similar form. Because the summation in $j$ in Eq. (6.66) is over all values, we can always carry out a transformation of $j$ of the form $j  \pm j + k$ where $k$ is an integer, provided that we apply it to the argument and its associated term. Also, since only cosines appear in the expansion we can always change the sign of the argument. We can then use these procedures to reduce the arguments in the expansion to some arbitrary standard form. In our case we have decided to make $j$ the coefficient of $\lambda ^ { \prime }$ in each argument.

As an example, consider the two terms in $e e ^ { \prime }$ in $\mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { D } } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ . These can be transformed to the same cosine argument,

$$
j \lambda ^ { \prime } - j \lambda + \varpi ^ { \prime } - \varpi ,
$$

by changing $j$ to $- j$ in the first, and then applying the transformation $j \to j + 1$ in each. The resulting term associated with this argument is then

$$
\frac { 1 } { 4 } e e ^ { \prime } \left[ 2 + 6 j + 4 j ^ { 2 } - 2 \alpha D - \alpha ^ { 2 } D ^ { 2 } \right] b _ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ^ { ( j + 1 ) } .
$$

Similar procedures can be carried out for the other arguments and the total number of arguments can be reduced from twenty-three to eleven. In such transformations we have made use of the fact that $b _ { s } ^ { ( - j ) } = b _ { s } ^ { ( j ) }$ , as given in Eq. (6.69). We also point out that, even with our decision to express all the arguments in a form where the coefficient of $\lambda ^ { \prime }$ is $j$ , the final form of our expansion is not unique and transformations of the form $j  - j$ followed by reversal of the argument produce arguments of a different form.

The final form of our second-order expansion of the direct part is

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \nabla _ { \perp \perp } \cdot \frac { \left( 1 - \theta \right) ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + \frac { \left( \theta - 2 \theta \right) ^ { 2 } } { 8 } \left( \theta ^ { 2 } - \theta ^ { 2 } \right) \left[ - 4 \theta ^ { 2 } + 2 \alpha D + \alpha ^ { 2 } \theta ^ { 2 } \right] ^ { 2 } \sqrt { \theta _ { \perp } ^ { \prime \prime } } } & { } \\ & { \quad + \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \epsilon ^ { 2 } - 2 \theta ^ { 2 } - 2 \theta \right) \left[ \epsilon \theta \right] ^ { 2 } \right) \epsilon \left( \alpha \left( \theta ^ { 2 } \theta ^ { 2 } \theta ^ { 2 } \right) - 1 \right) \right) } \\ & { \quad \times \cos \left( | \Omega ^ { \prime } - \partial \Omega | \right) } \\ & { = - \left( \frac { 1 } { 4 } \epsilon ^ { 2 } \left[ 2 + 6 \theta + 4 \theta ^ { 2 } - 2 2 \theta \theta - \theta ^ { 2 } \theta ^ { 2 } \right] \theta _ { \perp } ^ { \prime \prime } \right) } \\ & { \quad \times \cos \left( | \beta ^ { 2 } - \beta \phi + \theta ^ { 2 } - \pi \theta \right) } \\ & { \quad + \left( \epsilon ^ { \prime } | \alpha | \left( \theta ^ { 4 } \theta ^ { 2 } \right) \sin \left| \beta ^ { 2 } \right) \epsilon - \beta \epsilon + \Omega \right. } \\ & { \quad \left. + \left( \epsilon ^ { \prime } | \alpha | - 2 \theta ^ { 2 } - \alpha D | \right) \epsilon \left( \theta ^ { 2 } \theta ^ { 2 } \right) + \left( - 4 \theta - \theta \right) \right. } \\ & { \quad \left. + \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \epsilon ^ { 2 } - 2 \theta - 2 \theta \right) \left| \beta ^ { 2 } \right| \cos \left| \beta ^ { 2 } \right| \epsilon + \Omega - \beta \right| - \sigma \right| } \\ & { \quad + \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \epsilon ^ { 2 } - 1 + 2 \beta + \alpha D | \theta ^ { 2 } \right) \cosh \left( \beta ^ { 2 } \epsilon ^ { 2 } + ( 1 - \beta ) \lambda - \sigma \right) } \\ & { \quad - \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \epsilon ^ { 2 } - 2 \left( 1 + 2 \theta ^ { 2 } \right) \epsilon - 2 \alpha \right) + 4 \rho \left( \theta ^ { 2 } \theta ^ { 2 } \right) \left| \beta ^ { 2 } \right| } \\ &  = \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } \epsilon ^ { 2 } \left[ - 3 \end{array}
$$

Generating the indirect parts of the disturbing function defined in Eqs. (6.47) and (6.48) is relatively simple since we have already derived an expression for cos $\psi$ . Expressions for $r / a$ and $( a ^ { \prime } / r ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 }$ can be obtained from the elliptical expansions given in Sect. 2.5. To second order,

$$
{ \frac { r } { a } } = 1 - e \cos [ \lambda - \varpi ] + { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } e ^ { 2 } ( 1 - \cos [ 2 \lambda - 2 \varpi ] )
$$

and

$$
\left( { \frac { a ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime } } } \right) = 1 + 2 e ^ { \prime } \cos [ \lambda ^ { \prime } - \varpi ^ { \prime } ] + { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } e ^ { \prime 2 } \left( 1 + 5 \cos [ 2 \lambda ^ { \prime } - 2 \varpi ^ { \prime } ] \right)
$$

and hence

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { E } } = - \frac { r } { a } \left( \frac { a ^ { \prime } } { r ^ { \prime } } \right) ^ { 2 } \cos \psi } & { } \\ & { \approx \left( - 1 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { a ^ { \prime } } + s ^ { 2 } + s ^ { 2 } \right) \cos [ x ^ { \prime } - \lambda ] } \\ & { - e ^ { a } \cos [ 2 \lambda ^ { \prime } - 2 \lambda - \varpi ^ { \prime } + \varpi ] - 2 s ^ { \prime } \cos [ \lambda ^ { \prime } - \lambda - \Omega ^ { 2 } + \Omega ] } \\ & { - \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { \cos [ \lambda ^ { \prime } - 2 \lambda + \varpi ] } + \frac { 3 } { 2 } e ^ { \cos [ \lambda ^ { \prime } - \varpi ] } - 2 e ^ { \cos [ 2 \lambda ^ { \prime } - \lambda - \varpi ^ { \prime } ] } } \\ & { - \frac { 3 } { 8 } e ^ { 2 } \cos [ 2 \lambda ^ { \prime } - 3 \lambda + 2 \varpi ] - \frac { 1 } { 8 } e ^ { 2 } \cos [ \lambda ^ { \prime } + \lambda - 2 \varpi ] } \\ & { + 3 e ^ { a } \cos [ 2 \lambda - \varpi ^ { \prime } - \varpi ] - \frac { 1 } { 8 } e ^ { 2 } \cos [ \lambda ^ { \prime } + \lambda - 2 \varpi ^ { \prime } ] } \\ & { - \frac { 3 } { 2 } e ^ { 2 } \cos [ 3 ^ { \prime } - \lambda - 2 \varpi ^ { \prime } ] - s ^ { 2 } \cos [ 2 \lambda ^ { \prime } + \lambda - 2 \varpi ] } \\ & { + 2 s ^ { \prime } \cos [ 2 \lambda ^ { \prime } - \lambda - 2 \varpi ] - s ^ { 2 } \cos [ \lambda ^ { \prime } + \lambda - 2 \Omega ] } \\ & { + 2 s ^ { \prime } \cos [ 3 ^ { \prime } + \lambda - \Omega ^ { \prime } - 3 ] - 2 e ^ { 2 } \cos [ \lambda ^ { \prime } + \lambda - 2 \Omega ] , } \end{array}
$$

where we have changed the sign of the argument in some cases in order to adopt the same convention used to derive $\mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { D } }$ .

We can use similar methods to derive an expression for $\mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { I } }$ . Alternatively we can reverse the primed and unprimed quantities in our expression for $\mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { E } }$ . We obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { { \mathcal R } _ { 1 } = \frac { r } { a ^ { \prime } } \left( \frac { a } { r } \right) ^ { 2 } \cos \psi } \\ & { \quad \approx \left( - 1 + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \epsilon ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \epsilon ^ { 2 } + s ^ { 2 } + s ^ { 2 } \right) \cos [ \lambda ^ { \prime } - \lambda ] } \\ & { \quad \quad - e ^ { \epsilon } \cos [ 2 \lambda ^ { \prime } - 2 \lambda - \omega ^ { \prime } + 3 \omega ] - 2 s ^ { \prime } \cos [ \lambda ^ { \prime } - \lambda - \Omega ^ { \prime } + \Omega ] } \\ & { \quad \quad - 2 e \cos [ \lambda ^ { \prime } - 2 \lambda + \varpi ] + \frac { 3 } { 2 } e ^ { \epsilon } \cos [ \lambda - \varpi ] - \frac { 1 } { 2 } e ^ { \epsilon } \cos [ 2 \lambda ^ { \prime } - \lambda - \varpi ^ { \prime } ] } \\ & { \quad \quad - \frac { 2 7 } { 8 } \epsilon ^ { 2 } \cos [ \lambda ^ { \prime } - 3 \lambda + 2 \varpi ] - \frac { 1 } { 8 } e ^ { 2 } \cos [ \lambda ^ { \prime } + \lambda - 2 \varpi ] } \\ & { \quad \quad + 3 e ^ { \epsilon } \cos [ 2 \lambda - \varpi ^ { \prime } - \varpi ] - \frac { 1 } { 8 } e ^ { 2 } \cos [ \lambda ^ { \prime } + \lambda - 2 \varpi ] } \\ & { \quad \quad - \frac { 3 } { 8 } e ^ { 2 } \cos [ 3 \lambda ^ { \prime } - \lambda - 2 \varpi ] - s ^ { 2 } \cos [ 2 \lambda ^ { \prime } + \lambda - 2 \varpi ] } \\ & { \quad \quad + 2 s ^ { \prime } \cos [ \lambda ^ { \prime } + \lambda - 2 \Omega ^ { \prime } - \Omega ] - s ^ { 2 } \cos [ \lambda ^ { \prime } + \lambda - 2 \Omega ] . } \end{array}
$$

# 6.6 Terms Associated with a Specific Argument

The method outlined in Sect. 6.5 permits the calculation of a full expansion of the planetary disturbing function to any specified order in the eccentricities and inclinations. Its major disadvantage is that to find the terms associated with a specific argument one has to carry out a complete expansion to the order of that argument. However, in Sect. 6.3 we showed that in this respect there are distinct advantages to using Kaula’s form of the expansion. The major disadvantage of Kaula’s formulae is the lack of Laplace coefficients.

Ellis & Murray (1999) derived a variation on Kaula’s expansion that incorporates the best features of both approaches. Furthermore, they give explicit formulae for the finite series associated with a specific argument expanded to a specific order. Let the argument have the form

$$
\varphi = j _ { 1 } \lambda ^ { \prime } + j _ { 2 } \lambda + j _ { 3 } \varpi ^ { \prime } + j _ { 4 } \varpi + j _ { 5 } \Omega ^ { \prime } + j _ { 6 } \Omega
$$

and let $N _ { \mathrm { m a x } }$ be the maximum order of the expansion. Ellis & Murray showed that the expression for $\mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { D } }$ associated with $\varphi$ is

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \mathcal { R } } _ { \mathrm { D } } = \sum _ { i = 0 } ^ { i = 1 } \frac { ( 2 i ) ! } { i ! } \frac { ( - 1 ) ^ { i } } { 2 ^ { i + 1 } } a ^ { i } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad \times \sum _ { s = m - n = 0 } ^ { i } \frac { \nu \cos ( 2 s - 4 n + 1 ) ( s - n ) ! } { 2 ^ { ( 2 n } n ! ( 2 s - 2 n + 1 ) ! } \sum _ { n = 0 } ^ { s - 2 n } \frac { ( s - 2 n - m ) ! } { ( s - 2 n + m ) ! } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad \times ( - 1 ) ^ { s - 2 n - m } F _ { s - 2 n , n , p } ( D , F _ { s - 2 , n , n , p } ( T ) \sum _ { t = 0 } ^ { i - 1 } \frac { ( - 1 ) ^ { s } 2 ^ { t } } { ( t - s - D ) ! ! } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad \times \sum _ { \ell = 0 } ^ { \ell = 0 } \frac { ( - 1 ) ^ { t } } { \ell ! } \sum _ { k = 0 } ^ { \ell } \binom { t } { k } ( - 1 ) ^ { k } a ^ { \ell } \frac { \mathrm { d } ^ { \ell } } { \mathrm { d } \ell ^ { k } } b _ { t + \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ^ { ( 1 ) } ( \alpha ) } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad \times \sum _ { \ell = 0 } ^ { i - k } \frac { - 1 } { \ell - 1 - k - 2 k } ( \sigma ) L _ { \ell } ^ { - 1 } ( k + 1 + 1 ) . \mathrm { d } ^ { \ell } b _ { \ell } ( \ell ) } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad \times \cos [ j ] _ { \ell } \lambda ^ { \prime } + j \ge k . \mathrm { d } ^ { \ell } + j . } } \end{array}
$$

where, as before, $\kappa _ { 0 } = 1$ and $\kappa _ { m } = 2$ for $m \neq 0$ .

The following relationships hold throughout the calculation:

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { q = j _ { 4 } , } \\ & { \quad q ^ { \prime } = - j _ { 3 } , } \\ & { \ell _ { \mathrm { m a x } } = N _ { \mathrm { m a x } } - \left| j _ { 5 } \right| - \left| j _ { 6 } \right| , } \\ & { p _ { \mathrm { m i n } } = - ( j _ { 5 } + j _ { 6 } ) / 2 , \quad p _ { \mathrm { m i n } } ^ { \prime } = 0 \quad \mathrm { i f } \quad j _ { 5 } + j _ { 6 } < 0 , } \\ & { p _ { \mathrm { m i n } } = 0 , \quad p _ { \mathrm { m i n } } ^ { \prime } = ( j _ { 5 } + j _ { 6 } ) / 2 \quad \mathrm { i f } \quad j _ { 5 } + j _ { 6 } \geq 0 , } \\ & { s _ { \mathrm { m i n } } = \operatorname* { m a x } \left( p _ { \mathrm { m i n } } , \ p _ { \mathrm { m i n } } ^ { \prime } , \ j _ { 6 } + 2 p _ { \mathrm { m i n } } , \ - j _ { 5 } + 2 p _ { \mathrm { m i n } } ^ { \prime } \right) , } \\ & { i _ { \mathrm { m a x } } = [ ( N _ { \mathrm { m a x } } - \left| j _ { 3 } \right| - \left| j _ { 4 } \right| ) / 2 ] , } \end{array}
$$

where the square brackets in Eq. (6.120) denote the integer part of the expression. A number of intermediate definitions are required for the summation. These are:

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { n _ { \mathrm { m a x } } = \left[ \left( s - s _ { \mathrm { m i n } } \right) / 2 \right] , } \\ & { m _ { \mathrm { m i n } } = 0 \quad \mathrm { i f } \quad s , j _ { 5 } \quad \mathrm { a r e ~ b o t h ~ e v e n ~ o r ~ b o t h ~ o d d } , } \\ & { m _ { \mathrm { m i n } } = 1 \quad \mathrm { i f } \quad s , j _ { 5 } \quad \mathrm { a r e ~ n e i t h e r ~ b o t h ~ e v e n ~ n o r ~ b o t h ~ o d d } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { p = ( - j _ { 6 } - m + s - 2 n ) / 2 \quad \mathrm { w i t h ~ } p \leq s - 2 n \mathrm { ~ a n d ~ } p \geq p _ { \operatorname* { m i n } } , } \\ & { p ^ { \prime } = ( j _ { 5 } - m + s - 2 n ) / 2 \quad \mathrm { w i t h ~ } p ^ { \prime } \leq s - 2 n \mathrm { ~ a n d ~ } p ^ { \prime } \geq p _ { \operatorname* { m i n } } ^ { \prime } , } \\ & { \ j = \left| j _ { 2 } + i - 2 \bar { a } - 2 n - 2 p + q \right| . } \end{array}
$$

Note that $q$ and $q ^ { \prime }$ are determined directly from $\varphi$ and remain fixed over all the summations. However, $p$ and $p ^ { \prime }$ change with $s , n$ , and $m$ but the relationships given in Eqs. (6.124) and (6.125) always hold.

Ellis & Murray (1999) showed that the summations involved in the definitions of the functions of eccentricity and inclination in Eq. (6.113) need only be evaluated to a finite order that is at most equal to $N _ { \mathrm { m a x } }$ . The Hansen coefficient in $e$ need only include terms up to order $N _ { \mathrm { m a x } } - | j _ { 3 } | - | j _ { 5 } | - | j _ { 6 } |$ in $e$ ; similarly the Hansen coefficient in $e ^ { \prime }$ need only include terms up to order $N _ { \mathrm { m a x } } - | j _ { 4 } | - | j _ { 5 } | - | j _ { 6 } |$ in $e ^ { \prime }$ . The $F$ inclination function in $I$ need only include terms up to order $N _ { \mathrm { m a x } } - | j _ { 3 } | - | j _ { 4 } | - | j _ { 5 } |$ in $I$ ; similarly the $F$ function in $I ^ { \prime }$ need only include terms up to order $N _ { \mathrm { m a x } } - | j _ { 3 } | - | j _ { 4 } | - | j _ { 6 } |$ in $I ^ { \prime }$ .

For the indirect parts we have

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { E } } = - \kappa _ { m } \displaystyle \frac { ( 1 - m ) ! } { ( 1 + m ) ! } F _ { 1 , m , p } ( I ) F _ { 1 , m , p ^ { \prime } } ( I ^ { \prime } ) X _ { - j _ { 2 } } ^ { 1 , - j _ { 2 } - j _ { 4 } } ( e ) X _ { j _ { 1 } } ^ { - 2 , j _ { 1 } + j _ { 3 } } ( e ^ { \prime } ) } } \\ { { \times \cos \left[ j _ { 1 } \lambda ^ { \prime } + j _ { 2 } \lambda + j _ { 3 } \varpi ^ { \prime } + j _ { 4 } \varpi + j _ { 5 } \Omega ^ { \prime } + j _ { 6 } \Omega \right] } } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \mathcal { R } } _ { \mathrm { I } } = - \kappa _ { m } \displaystyle \frac { ( 1 - m ) ! } { ( 1 + m ) ! } F _ { 1 , m , p } ( I ) F _ { 1 , m , p ^ { \prime } } ( I ^ { \prime } ) X _ { - j _ { 2 } } ^ { - 2 , - j _ { 2 } - j _ { 4 } } ( e ) X _ { j _ { 1 } } ^ { 1 , j _ { 1 } + j _ { 3 } } ( e ^ { \prime } ) } \\ { \times \cos \left[ j _ { 1 } \lambda ^ { \prime } + j _ { 2 } \lambda + j _ { 3 } \varpi ^ { \prime } + j _ { 4 } \varpi + j _ { 5 } \Omega ^ { \prime } + j _ { 6 } \Omega \right] , } \end{array}
$$

where each of the quantities $p , \ p ^ { \prime }$ , and $m$ must be integers and equal to 0 or 1. If these conditions are not satisfied then the given argument does not appear in the expansion of the indirect part. As with $\mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { D } }$ we can reduce the extent of the series expansions in powers of the eccentricity and inclination, and the same modifications apply. An analysis of the integers involved in the expansion of this indirect part gives the following relationships:

$$
\begin{array} { l } { q = j _ { 4 } , } \\ { q ^ { \prime } = - j _ { 3 } , } \\ { p = ( j _ { 2 } + j _ { 4 } + 1 ) / 2 , } \\ { p ^ { \prime } = - ( j _ { 1 } + j _ { 3 } - 1 ) / 2 , } \\ { m = j _ { 5 } - 2 p ^ { \prime } + 1 . } \end{array}
$$

# 6.7 Use of the Disturbing Function

The complete expansions for $\mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { D } } , \mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { E } }$ , and $\mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { I } }$ contain an infinite number of cosine arguments. However, in practice we are only interested in certain cosine arguments and we can neglect all others. Our basis for doing this is the averaging principle whereby we assume (with some justification) that all the unimportant terms will be of short period and therefore their effects will average out to zero over the longer-period motion. This concept is illustrated in Sect. 6.9. All that concerns us here is that the averaging principle allows us to isolate those terms in the disturbing function that are appropriate for a particular problem and to ignore the infinite number of remaining terms. Effectively we move from a consideration of the infinite series of the full disturbing function, $\mathcal { R }$ , to a finite series of the averaged disturbing function, $\langle \mathcal { R } \rangle$ . This concept is the basis of our analysis of secular perturbations in Chapter 7, resonant perturbations in Chapter 8, and their applications to chaotic motion in Chapter 9 and planetary rings in Chapter 10. This approach to the use of the planetary disturbing function permits us to carry out analytical studies when we move beyond the simplicity of the two-body problem.

The procedure for determining the appropriate term, $\langle \mathcal { R } \rangle$ or $\langle \mathcal { R } ^ { \prime } \rangle$ , in the disturbing function is as follows:

1) Decide which combination of angles, $\varphi$ , is applicable to the problem at hand. This requires knowledge of the physical problem and will be discussed in Sect. 6.9.   
2) Determine the “order”, $N$ , of the argument. This is equal to the absolute value of the sum of the coefficients of $\lambda$ and $\lambda ^ { \prime }$ in $\varphi$ .   
3) By looking at the appropriate order terms in the expansion of $\mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { D } }$ , determine the value of the integer $j$ that gives agreement with the desired argument, $\varphi$ .   
4) Calculate the combination of Laplace coefficients for that value of $j$ to give the explicit form of the term of interest, $\langle \mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { D } } \rangle$ say.   
5) Decide whether an external or an internal perturbation is being considered. This is determined by the nature of the problem.   
6) If the perturbation is external, then look at the appropriate order terms in the expansion of the indirect part, $\mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { E } }$ , and isolate a matching argument, if it exists, and read off the corresponding indirect term $\langle \mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { E } } \rangle$ .   
7) If the perturbation is internal, then look at the appropriate order terms in the expansion of the indirect part, $\mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { I } }$ , and isolate a matching argument, if it exists, and read off the corresponding indirect term $\langle \mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { I } } \rangle$ .

8) If the perturbation is external then

$$
\langle \mathcal { R } \rangle = \frac { \mu ^ { \prime } } { a ^ { \prime } } \left( \langle \mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { D } } \rangle + \alpha \langle \mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { E } } \rangle \right) .
$$

9) If the perturbation is internal then

$$
\langle \mathcal { R } ^ { \prime } \rangle = \frac { \mu } { a } \left( \alpha \langle \mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { D } } \rangle + \frac { 1 } { \alpha } \langle \mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { I } } \rangle \right) .
$$

Use of the explicit expansion of the indirect part in steps 6 and 7 can be avoided altogether since the averaged indirect part of the disturbing function, $\langle \mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { E } } \rangle$ or $\langle \mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { I } } \rangle$ , can be obtained from the averaged direct part, $\langle \mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { D } } \rangle$ . The procedure is as follows: To obtain $\langle \mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { E } } \rangle$ replace every occurrence of $\alpha ^ { n } D ^ { n } A _ { 1 }$ $( n = 0 , 1 )$ in $\langle \mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { D } } \rangle$ by $^ { - 1 }$ and replace every occurrence of $\alpha ^ { n } D ^ { n } B _ { 0 }$ $( n = 0 , 1 )$ ) in $\langle \mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { D } } \rangle$ by $^ { - 2 }$ ; all other terms are ignored. To obtain $\langle \mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { I } } \rangle$ replace every occurrence of $\alpha ^ { n } D ^ { n } A _ { 1 }$ $( n = 0 , 1 , 2 , . . . )$ in $\langle \mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { D } } \rangle$ by $( - 1 ) ^ { n + 1 } ( n + 1 ) !$ and replace every occurrence of $\alpha ^ { n } D ^ { n } B _ { 0 }$ $( n = 0 , 1 , 2 , . . . )$ in $\langle \mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { D } } \rangle$ by $( - 1 ) ^ { n + 1 } ( 2 n + 2 ) n !$ , ignoring all other terms.

Throughout this analysis we have assumed that $r ^ { \prime } > r$ (i.e., that the orbits do not intersect). The convergence of the resulting series will therefore depend on how close the orbits are to intersection. Obviously if the orbits intersect there will be a singularity since $\mathbf { r } = \mathbf { r } ^ { \prime }$ at some longitude and the first term in Eq. (6.16) or (6.18) becomes undefined. Hence an approximate condition for convergence is

$$
a ( 1 + e ) < a ^ { \prime } ( 1 - e ^ { \prime } ) ,
$$

or that the apocentric distance of the inner orbit has to be less than the pericentric distance of the outer orbit.

Another advantage of the Legendre-type expansion given in Eq. (6.113) is that it is easy to see the form of the lowest order terms in the expansion. We have already stated in Sect. 6.3 (and shown in Sect. 6.5) that the potential associated with the perturbations of the orbit of the mass $m$ by the mass $m ^ { \prime }$ can be written as

$$
\mathcal { R } = \mu ^ { \prime } \sum S \cos \varphi ,
$$

where $s$ is a function of the semi-major axes, eccentricities, and inclinations of $m$ and $m ^ { \prime }$ . From the definitions of mean longitude and longitude of pericentre, the general form of the argument $\varphi$ can be written as

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \varphi = ( l - 2 p ^ { \prime } + q ^ { \prime } ) \lambda ^ { \prime } - ( l - 2 p + q ) \lambda - q ^ { \prime } \varpi ^ { \prime } + q \varpi } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { + ( m - l + 2 p ^ { \prime } ) \Omega ^ { \prime } - ( m - l + 2 p ) \Omega , } } \end{array}
$$

where, in this case, $l , m , p , p ^ { \prime } , q$ , and $q ^ { \prime }$ are all integers. We can calculate the valid arguments by using the property that the sum of the integer coefficients of the angle variables in each argument is zero. If we write the general form of an argument as

$$
\varphi = j _ { 1 } \lambda ^ { \prime } + j _ { 2 } \lambda + j _ { 3 } \varpi ^ { \prime } + j _ { 4 } \varpi + j _ { 5 } \Omega ^ { \prime } + j _ { 6 } \Omega
$$

then our condition on the coefficients implies that

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 6 } j _ { i } = 0 .
$$

This is the d’Alembert relation and it does not apply to any choice of angles – we must use angles that are referred to a fixed direction (i.e., longitudes rather than anomalies). The longitudes $\lambda , \lambda ^ { \prime } , \varpi , \varpi ^ { \prime } , \Omega$ , and $\Omega ^ { \prime }$ form an appropriate set of angles. Hamilton (1994) provides an overview of the d’Alembert rules that determine such relationships.

Now consiproperties of m of and $s$ “strength” of an individual term. From thewe can calculate the lowest order terms in $X _ { l - 2 p + q } ^ { l , l - 2 p } ( e )$ $F _ { l m p } ( I )$ the eccentricities and inclinations. Use of Eqs. (6.37)–(6.42) gives

$$
X _ { l - 2 p + q } ^ { l , l - 2 p } ( e ) = \mathcal { O } ( e ^ { | q | } ) , \qquad X _ { l - 2 p ^ { \prime } + q ^ { \prime } } ^ { - l - 1 , l - 2 p ^ { \prime } } ( e ^ { \prime } ) = \mathcal { O } ( e ^ { \prime | q ^ { \prime } | } )
$$

and

$$
F _ { l m p } ( I ) = \mathcal { O } ( s ^ { | m - l + 2 p | } ) , \qquad F _ { l m p ^ { \prime } } ( I ^ { \prime } ) = \mathcal { O } ( s ^ { \prime | m - l + 2 p ^ { \prime } | } ) ,
$$

where $s = \sin { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } I$ and $s ^ { \prime } = \sin { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } I ^ { \prime }$ . Therefore we can write

$$
S \approx \frac { f ( \alpha ) } { a ^ { \prime } } e ^ { | q | } e ^ { \prime | q ^ { \prime } | } s ^ { | m - l + 2 p | } s ^ { \prime | m - l + 2 p ^ { \prime } | } = \frac { f ( \alpha ) } { a ^ { \prime } } e ^ { | j _ { 4 } | } e ^ { \prime | j _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } | } s ^ { | j _ { 6 } | } s ^ { \prime | j _ { 5 } | } ,
$$

where $f ( \alpha )$ can be expressed as a function of Laplace coefficients. Hence the lowest power of $e$ , for example, in a given term is at least equal to the absolute value of the coefficient of $\varpi$ . Similarly the lowest powers of $e ^ { \prime }$ , $\sin { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } I$ , and sin $\frac { 1 } { 2 } I ^ { \prime }$ are greater than or equal to the absolute value of the coefficients of $\varpi ^ { \prime } , \Omega$ , and $\Omega ^ { \prime }$ respectively in $\varphi$ . This property is clear from the second-order expansion given in Sect. 6.5 and the fourth-order expansion in Appendix B.

# 6.8 Lagrange’s Planetary Equations

The expansion of the disturbing function gives us the dependence of the perturbing potential on the orbital elements. Now we need to quantify the resulting orbital variations of the perturbed body. To do this we make use Lagrange’s planetary equations. These are best derived using a Hamiltonian formulation (see Sect. 2.10). Here we confine ourselves to a statement of the equations. Full derivations can be found in Brouwer & Clemence (1961) and Roy (1988).

The use of Lagrange’s equations require the introduction of an additional angle. If we write

$$
\lambda = M + \varpi = n ( t - \tau ) + \varpi = n t + \epsilon ,
$$

where $\lambda$ is the mean longitude, $M$ is the mean anomaly, $\varpi$ is the longitude of pericentre, $t$ is time, and $\tau$ is the time of pericentre passage, then the new angle $\epsilon$ denotes the mean longitude at epoch (i.e., the mean longitude of the mass $m$ at the moment from which time is measured). Lagrange’s equations for the variations of the orbital elements are

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \displaystyle \frac { \mathrm { d } a } { \mathrm { d } t } = \frac { 2 } { n a } \frac { \partial \mathcal { R } } { \partial \epsilon } , } \\ & { \displaystyle \frac { \mathrm { d } e } { \mathrm { d } t } = - \frac { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } { n a ^ { 2 } e } ( 1 - \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } ) \frac { \partial \mathcal { R } } { \partial \epsilon } - \frac { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } { n a ^ { 2 } e } \frac { \partial \mathcal { R } } { \partial \varpi } , } \\ & { \displaystyle \frac { \mathrm { d } \epsilon } { \mathrm { d } t } = - \frac { 2 } { n a } \frac { \partial R } { \partial a } + \frac { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } ( 1 - \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } ) } { n a ^ { 2 } e } \frac { \partial \mathcal { R } } { \partial e } + \frac { \tan \frac { 1 } { 2 } I } { n a ^ { 2 } \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } \frac { \partial \mathcal { R } } { \partial I } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { \mathrm { d } \Omega } { \mathrm { d } t } = \frac { 1 } { n a ^ { 2 } \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } \sin { I } } \frac { \partial \mathcal { R } } { \partial I } , } \\ & { \frac { \mathrm { d } \varpi } { \mathrm { d } t } = \frac { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } { n a ^ { 2 } e } \frac { \partial \mathcal { R } } { \partial e } + \frac { \tan { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } I } { n a ^ { 2 } \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } \frac { \partial \mathcal { R } } { \partial I } , } \\ & { \frac { \mathrm { d } I } { \mathrm { d } t } = \frac { - \tan { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } I } { n a ^ { 2 } \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } \left( \frac { \partial \mathcal { R } } { \partial \epsilon } + \frac { \partial \mathcal { R } } { \partial \varpi } \right) - \frac { 1 } { n a ^ { 2 } \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } \sin { I } } \frac { \partial \mathcal { R } } { \partial \Omega } . } \end{array}
$$

A problem arises if we consider the expression for $\dot { \epsilon }$ given in Eq. (6.147) (see, e.g., Brouwer & Clemence 1961). Since the right-hand side of the equation contains a factor $\partial \mathcal { R } / \partial a$ we have to be aware that the semi-major axis occurs explicitly in the Laplace coefficients of the disturbing function and implicitly in the arguments of the cosine terms as the mean motion since $\lambda = n t + \epsilon$ . This gives rise to the time occurring as a factor when the partial derivative is taken. The problem can be overcome if we define a new mean longitude at epoch, $\epsilon ^ { * }$ , by

$$
\frac { \mathrm { d } \epsilon ^ { * } } { \mathrm { d } t } = \frac { \mathrm { d } \epsilon } { \mathrm { d } t } + t \frac { \mathrm { d } n } { \mathrm { d } t } .
$$

Hence

$$
\frac { \mathrm { d } \lambda } { \mathrm { d } t } = n + \frac { \mathrm { d } \epsilon ^ { * } } { \mathrm { d } t }
$$

and

$$
\lambda = \int n \mathrm { d } t + \epsilon ^ { * } .
$$

This can also be written as

$$
\lambda = \rho + \epsilon ^ { * } ,
$$

where

$$
{ \frac { \mathrm { d } \rho } { \mathrm { d } t } } = n , \qquad { \frac { \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \rho } { \mathrm { d } t ^ { 2 } } } = { \frac { \mathrm { d } n } { \mathrm { d } t } } = - { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } { \frac { n } { a } } { \frac { \mathrm { d } a } { \mathrm { d } t } }
$$

or

$$
\frac { \mathrm { d } ^ { 2 } \rho } { \mathrm { d } t ^ { 2 } } = - \frac { 3 } { a ^ { 2 } } \frac { \partial \mathcal { R } } { \partial \epsilon } .
$$

In this case we should consider any derivatives of $\partial / \partial \epsilon$ , such as those that occur in the expressions for ${ \dot { a } } , { \dot { e } } .$ , and $\dot { I }$ , to mean $\partial / \partial \lambda$ . In practice the variation of $\epsilon$ can usually be neglected since it is a small effect.

The variation of the orbital elements of the mass $m ^ { \prime }$ can be expressed by equations similar to Eqs. (6.145)–(6.150), with $\mathcal { R }$ replaced by $\mathcal { R } ^ { \prime }$ and all unprimed variables exchanged for primed ones. The derivation of Lagrange’s planetary equations does not assume that $\mathcal { R }$ arises from perturbations by an external mass. Therefore we can equally well use these equations to study the perturbations on the mass $m$ due to, for example, a nonspherical central mass. This will be considered in Sect. 6.11. Similarly the equations are equally applicable if we use the averaged disturbing functions $\langle \mathcal { R } \rangle$ and $\langle \mathcal { R } ^ { \prime } \rangle$ .

We have already seen in Sect. 2.9 that the variations in the orbital elements can be expressed in terms of the radial, tangential, and orthogonal forces acting on an orbiting object. However, Lagrange’s equations allow us to derive similar variations but based on the Fourier series expansion of the disturbing function discussed in this chapter. As such they provide the basis for most of the perturbation calculations that follow.

# 6.9 Classification of Arguments in the Disturbing Function

We can now approach the subject of the physical significance of the expansion of the disturbing function. So far we have expressed the perturbing potential as a series involving an infinite number of permissible combinations of angles. But which angles are important in any given problem? In other words, which of the infinite terms in the expansion are important and which can be ignored? To a large extent the answers to these questions depend on the semi-major axis of the perturbed orbit. We can classify all arguments by considering the frequencies or periods associated with the cosine arguments in the expansion.

Each cosine argument contains a linear combination of the angles $\lambda ^ { \prime } , \lambda , \varpi ^ { \prime }$ , $\varpi , \Omega ^ { \prime }$ , and $\Omega$ . We know that in the unperturbed problem the mean longitudes, $\lambda ^ { \prime }$ and $\lambda$ , increase linearly at rates $n ^ { \prime }$ and $n$ respectively. In contrast, all the other angles are constant in the unperturbed problem. Therefore, when we consider the perturbed system $\lambda ^ { \prime }$ and $\lambda$ are rapidly varying quantities, whereas all the other angles undergo slow variations. Therefore, any valid arguments that do not involve mean longitudes are slowly varying. These give rise to secular terms, from the Latin verb saeculum meaning century, or long period. This does not imply that all other arguments are of short period. Consider a general argument of the form $\varphi = j _ { 1 } \lambda ^ { \prime } + j _ { 2 } \lambda + j _ { 3 } \varpi ^ { \prime } + j _ { 4 } \varpi + j _ { 5 } \Omega ^ { \prime } + j _ { 6 } \Omega$ with

$$
\lambda ^ { \prime } \approx n ^ { \prime } t + \epsilon ^ { \prime } \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad \lambda \approx n t + \epsilon
$$

(see Eq. (6.144)). Therefore $j _ { 1 } \lambda ^ { \prime } + j _ { 2 } \lambda \approx ( j _ { 1 } n ^ { \prime } + j _ { 2 } n ) t + c$ onstant and so, if the semi-major axes are such that

$$
j _ { 1 } n ^ { \prime } + j _ { 2 } n \approx 0 ,
$$

then this argument also has a period longer than either orbital period. Equation (6.158) is satisfied when there is a commensurability between the two mean motions or orbital periods (see Sect. 1.7). We classify such arguments as giving rise to resonant terms in the expansion. If we consider the semi-major axes, the equivalent condition is

$$
a \approx ( | j _ { 1 } | / | j _ { 2 } | ) ^ { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } a ^ { \prime } .
$$

Because of the dependence on semi-major axis, resonant terms are localised. Whereas a particular combination of angles may be slowly varying at one semimajor axis of the perturbed body, the same combination would be varying rapidly at another. In contrast the secular terms can be considered as global.

Any argument that is neither secular nor resonant is considered to give rise to a short-period term. In practise the application of the averaging principle mentioned in Sect. 6.7 allows us to ignore the infinite number of short-period terms in the expansion and accept that the dynamics is dominated by the appropriate secular and resonant terms.

Below we provide predictions of motion under secular and resonant terms in the context of the elliptical restricted three-body problem with small inclination, and we compare the answers with the results of numerical integrations. Here we assume that the mass $m$ is negligible and that the orbit of $m ^ { \prime }$ is a fixed ellipse in the reference plane. Our starting point is a set of the lowest order form of Lagrange’s equations for ${ \dot { a } } , { \dot { e } } , { \dot { \varpi } }$ , and $\dot { \Omega }$ derived from inspection of Eqs. (6.145), (6.146), (6.149), and (6.148). The equations of motion are

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { \mathrm { d } a } { \mathrm { d } t } = \cfrac { 2 } { n a } \frac { \partial \langle \mathcal { R } \rangle } { \partial \lambda } , } \\ & { \frac { \mathrm { d } e } { \mathrm { d } t } = - \cfrac { 1 } { n a ^ { 2 } e } \frac { \partial \langle \mathcal { R } \rangle } { \partial \varpi } , } \\ & { \frac { \mathrm { d } \varpi } { \mathrm { d } t } = + \cfrac { 1 } { n a ^ { 2 } e } \frac { \partial \langle \mathcal { R } \rangle } { \partial e } , } \\ & { \frac { \mathrm { d } \Omega } { \mathrm { d } t } = + \cfrac { 1 } { n a ^ { 2 } \sin I } \cfrac { \partial \langle \mathcal { R } \rangle } { \partial I } , } \end{array}
$$

where $\langle \mathcal { R } \rangle$ is the averaged part of the disturbing function for an external perturber.

# 6.9.1 Secular Terms

Secular terms arise from those arguments that do not contain the mean longitudes. Inspection of the direct part of the second-order expansion in Eq. (6.107) shows that secular terms are obtained by setting $j = 0$ in those cosine arguments containing $j \lambda ^ { \prime } - j \lambda$ . This gives

$$
\langle \mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { D } } \rangle = C _ { 0 } + C _ { 1 } ( e ^ { 2 } + e ^ { \prime 2 } ) + C _ { 2 } s ^ { 2 } + C _ { 3 } e e ^ { \prime } \cos ( \varpi ^ { \prime } - \varpi ) ,
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle C _ { 0 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } b _ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ^ { ( 0 ) } ( \alpha ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle C _ { 1 } = \frac { 1 } { 8 } \left[ 2 \alpha D + \alpha ^ { 2 } D ^ { 2 } \right] b _ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ^ { ( 0 ) } ( \alpha ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle C _ { 2 } = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \alpha b _ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } ^ { ( 1 ) } ( \alpha ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle C _ { 3 } = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left[ 2 - 2 \alpha D - \alpha ^ { 2 } D ^ { 2 } \right] b _ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ^ { ( 1 ) } ( \alpha ) . } } \end{array}
$$

Note that there are no $s s ^ { \prime }$ or $s ^ { \prime 2 }$ terms in $\langle \mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { D } } \rangle$ because we are taking $s ^ { \prime } = 0$ and that $C _ { 0 }$ is a function of $\alpha$ only. Furthermore, inspection of the terms in $\mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { E } }$ (Eq. (6.110)) shows that all the arguments contain at least one mean longitude and so there are no secular contributions from the indirect part of the disturbing function. Hence the low-order version of Lagrange’s equations becomes

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \left( \frac { \mathrm { d } a } { \mathrm { d } t } \right) _ { \mathrm { s e c } } = 0 , } \\ & { \left( \frac { \mathrm { d } e } { \mathrm { d } t } \right) _ { \mathrm { s e c } } = n \alpha ( m ^ { \prime } / m _ { \mathrm { c } } ) C _ { 3 } e ^ { \prime } \sin ( \varpi - \varpi ^ { \prime } ) , } \\ & { \left( \frac { \mathrm { d } \varpi } { \mathrm { d } t } \right) _ { \mathrm { s e c } } = n \alpha ( m ^ { \prime } / m _ { \mathrm { c } } ) \left[ 2 C _ { 1 } + C _ { 3 } ( e ^ { \prime } / e ) \cos ( \varpi - \varpi ^ { \prime } ) \right] , } \\ & { \left( \frac { \mathrm { d } \Omega } { \mathrm { d } t } \right) _ { \mathrm { s e c } } = n \alpha ( m ^ { \prime } / m _ { \mathrm { c } } ) ( C _ { 2 } / 2 ) , } \end{array}
$$

where we have used the fact that $\mu ^ { \prime } = \mathcal { G } m ^ { \prime } \approx n ^ { 2 } a ^ { 3 } ( m ^ { \prime } / m _ { \mathrm { c } } )$ , where $m _ { \mathrm { c } }$ is the mass of the central object. If we assume that $e \gg e ^ { \prime }$ then the approximate solutions to these equations are

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { a = a _ { 0 } , } } \\ { { e = e _ { 0 } - \displaystyle \frac { n \alpha } { \dot { \varpi } } ( m ^ { \prime } / m _ { \mathrm { c } } ) C _ { 3 } e ^ { \prime } \left[ \cos { \varpi _ { 0 } } - \cos { \varpi } \right] , } } \\ { { \varpi = \varpi _ { 0 } + n \alpha ( m ^ { \prime } / m _ { \mathrm { c } } ) 2 C _ { 1 } t , } } \\ { { \Omega = \Omega _ { 0 } + n \alpha ( m ^ { \prime } / m _ { \mathrm { c } } ) ( C _ { 2 } / 2 ) t , } } \end{array}
$$

where the subscript 0 denotes the initial $( t = 0$ ) value of a quantity, and we have taken $\varpi ^ { \prime } = 0$ . These solutions predict that there is no secular change in $a$ , that $e$ varies sinusoidally with an amplitude of

$$
( \Delta e ) _ { \mathrm { s e c } } = \left| ( n \alpha / \dot { \varpi } ) ( m ^ { \prime } / m _ { \mathrm { c } } ) C _ { 3 } e ^ { \prime } \right| ,
$$

and that $\varpi$ and $\Omega$ will either increase or decrease linearly with time depending on the signs of $C _ { 1 }$ and $C _ { 2 }$ .

Figures 6.3a–d show the results of a numerical integration of the full equations of motion of the elliptical restricted three-body problem with $a ^ { \prime } = 1$ , $e ^ { \prime } = 0 . 0 4 8$ , $\varpi ^ { \prime } = 0$ , $I ^ { \prime } = 0$ , and $m ^ { \prime } / m _ { \mathrm { c } } = 1 / 1 0 4 7 . 3 5 5$ with starting conditions $a _ { 0 } = 0 . 1 9 2$ , $e _ { 0 } ~ = ~ 0 . 1$ , $\varpi _ { 0 } ~ = ~ 1 3 0 ^ { \circ }$ , $\Omega _ { 0 } ~ = ~ 2 0 0 ^ { \circ }$ , $\lambda _ { 0 } ~ = ~ 3 0 0 ^ { \circ }$ , and $\lambda ^ { \prime } = 0 ^ { \circ }$ . Substitution of $\alpha = a / a ^ { \prime } = 0 . 1 9 2$ in Eqs. (6.166)–(6.168) gives $C _ { 1 } = 0 . 0 1 4 8 3 3 5$ , $C _ { 2 } =$ $- 0 . 0 5 9 3 3 3 9$ , and $C _ { 3 } = - 0 . 0 0 7 0 8 6 8 8$ ; note that $2 C _ { 1 } = - C _ { 2 } / 2$ . Since the mass ratio is that of the Jupiter–Sun ratio, the integration was designed to mimic the motion of an asteroid perturbed by Jupiter, and so the time units in the plots are given as Jupiter periods. However, the semi-major axis was deliberately chosen to be far away from Jupiter in order to avoid proximity to strong resonances. In these circumstances the secular perturbations alone should provide a good approximation to the motion.

![](images/c82fdd226df8994598b2f24ad608c75b916f4b60de4969457097f911ca2eed4a.jpg)  
Fig. 6.3. A comparison of the results of a full numerical integration (thick line) with predictions from analytical theory (thin line) for the variation of (a) semi-major axis, (b) eccentricity, (c) longitude of perihelion, and (d) longitude of ascending node for a test particle undergoing predominantly secular perturbations from Jupiter.

The results show that the agreement is excellent over the 20,000 Jupiter periods of the integration. There are variations in $a$ but these are extremely small; note that the scale in Fig. 6.3a is enlarged. The fact that the semi-major axis is almost constant justifies the evaluation of the Laplace coefficients for a fixed value of $\alpha$ . The eccentricity does vary as predicted, and while $\varpi$ increases linearly with time (since $C _ { 1 } > 0$ ), $\Omega$ is decreasing linearly at the same rate (since $2 C _ { 1 } = - C _ { 2 } / 2$ ; cf. Eqs. (6.175) and (6.176)). Prograde motion of the pericentre (or node) is called precession and retrograde motion is called regression. The behaviour of $\varpi$ and $\Omega$ is a natural consequence of the secular terms in the disturbing function.

Because of the infinite number of short-period terms in the disturbing function, which we have neglected, there should be differences between the results of a full integration and the predictions of our analytical theory. We can see this already in the Fig. 6.3a, where there are small, but detectable short-period changes in the semi-major axis from the constant value predicted by theory. Figure 6.4 shows the difference between the “observed” eccentricity (i.e., the one determined by the numerical integration) and the calculated value from theory, as a function of time for the first 1,000 Jupiter periods of the integration. Here again we can see the effect of the short-period terms inherently included in any full integration.

![](images/58ef9c2735bf830e5b3f6521053d1a2b5778f69c3160d2b6fe1fc18cd47b8349.jpg)  
Fig. 6.4. Differences between the observed and calculated values of the test particle’s eccentricity as a function of time. The data are sampled every Jupiter period and show the short-period variations in $e$ .

# 6.9.2 Resonant Terms

Now suppose, for example, that we want to study an asteroid’s motion at 3.27 AU, under the perturbing effect of Jupiter. Since Jupiter’s semi-major axis is 5.20 AU we have, using Kepler’s third law, that the ratio of their periods is $( 3 . 2 7 / 5 . 2 0 ) ^ { 3 / 2 } \approx$ 0.499. Hence, we have the relation $2 n ^ { \prime } \approx n$ and we would expect resonant terms to be important. Therefore, in the vicinity of the 2:1 resonance, as well as the secular terms discussed above, we also need to consider those terms in the expansion of the disturbing function that contain $2 \lambda ^ { \prime } - \lambda$ (i.e., the resonant terms for this location).

Inspection of Eq. (6.107) shows that in a second-order expansion there are two terms in $\langle \mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { D } } \rangle / a ^ { \prime }$ that have a cosine argument containing $2 \lambda ^ { \prime } - \lambda$ for specific values of $j$ . The relevant direct part of the averaged disturbing function is

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { \langle \mathcal { R } _ { \mathrm { D } } \rangle = C _ { 0 } + C _ { 1 } ( e ^ { 2 } + e ^ { \prime 2 } ) + C _ { 2 } ( s ^ { 2 } + s ^ { \prime 2 } ) + C _ { 3 } e e ^ { \prime } \cos ( \varpi - \varpi ^ { \prime } ) } \\ & { } & { + \ : C _ { 4 } e \cos ( 2 \lambda ^ { \prime } - \lambda - \varpi ) + C _ { 5 } e ^ { \prime } \cos ( 2 \lambda ^ { \prime } - \lambda - \varpi ^ { \prime } ) , } \end{array}
$$

where the additional constants $C _ { 4 }$ and $C _ { 5 }$ are given by

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { C _ { 4 } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ - 4 - \alpha D \right] b _ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ^ { ( 2 ) } ( \alpha ) , } } \\ { { C _ { 5 } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ 3 + \alpha D \right] b _ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ^ { ( 1 ) } ( \alpha ) . } } \end{array}
$$

The second of these two resonant arguments makes no contribution to ${ \dot { e } } , { \dot { \varpi } }$ , and $\dot { \Omega }$ but does contribute a term to $\dot { a }$ . Inspection of Eq. (6.110) shows that there is also a $- 2 \alpha e ^ { \prime }$ contribution to the same argument from the indirect part.

Application of the approximate form of Lagrange’s equations gives

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \left( \frac { \mathrm { d } a } { \mathrm { d } t } \right) _ { \mathrm { r e s } } = 2 n \alpha a ( m ^ { \prime } / m _ { \mathrm { c } } ) C _ { 4 } e \sin ( 2 \lambda ^ { \prime } - \lambda - \varpi ) } \\ & { \qquad + 2 n \alpha a ( m ^ { \prime } / m _ { \mathrm { c } } ) \left( C _ { 5 } - 2 \alpha \right) e ^ { \prime } \sin ( 2 \lambda ^ { \prime } - \lambda - \varpi ^ { \prime } ) , } \\ & { \left( \frac { \mathrm { d } e } { \mathrm { d } t } \right) _ { \mathrm { r e s } } = n \alpha ( m ^ { \prime } / m _ { \mathrm { c } } ) C _ { 4 } \sin ( 2 \lambda ^ { \prime } - \lambda - \varpi ) , } \\ & { \left( \frac { \mathrm { d } \varpi } { \mathrm { d } t } \right) _ { \mathrm { r e s } } = n \alpha ( m ^ { \prime } / m _ { \mathrm { c } } ) ( C _ { 4 } / e ) \cos ( 2 \lambda ^ { \prime } - \lambda - \varpi ) , } \\ & { \left( \frac { \mathrm { d } \Omega } { \mathrm { d } t } \right) _ { \mathrm { r e s } } = 0 } \end{array}
$$

for the variations in $a , e , \varpi$ , and $\Omega$ due to the 2:1 resonance. If we consider approximate solutions for these resonant equations alone we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { a = a _ { 0 } - { \displaystyle \frac { 2 n \alpha a ( m ^ { \prime } / m _ { \mathrm { c } } ) C _ { 4 } e } { 2 n ^ { \prime } - n - \bar { w } } } \left[ \cos ( 2 \lambda ^ { \prime } - \lambda - \varpi ) - \cos ( \lambda _ { 0 } + \omega _ { 0 } ) \right] } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } - { \displaystyle \frac { 2 n \alpha a ( m ^ { \prime } / m _ { \mathrm { c } } ) ( C _ { 5 } - 2 \alpha ) e ^ { \prime } } { 2 n ^ { \prime } - n } } \left[ \cos ( 2 \lambda ^ { \prime } - \lambda - \varpi ^ { \prime } ) - \cos \lambda _ { 0 } \right] , } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } { } { \displaystyle { e = e _ { 0 } + { \displaystyle \frac { n \alpha ( m ^ { \prime } / m _ { \mathrm { c } } ) C _ { 4 } } { 2 n ^ { \prime } - n - \bar { w } } } \left[ \cos ( 2 \lambda ^ { \prime } - \lambda - \varpi ) - \cos ( \lambda _ { 0 } + \omega _ { 0 } ) \right] } , } } \\ { { \varpi = \varpi _ { 0 } + { \displaystyle \frac { n \alpha ( m ^ { \prime } / m _ { \mathrm { c } } ) ( C _ { 4 } / e ) } { 2 n ^ { \prime } - n - \bar { \omega } } } \left[ \sin ( 2 \lambda ^ { \prime } - \lambda - \varpi ) + \sin ( \lambda _ { 0 } + \omega _ { 0 } ) \right] , } } \\ { { \Omega = \Omega _ { 0 } . } } \end{array}
$$

To derive these solutions we have assumed that the only time-varying quantities on the right-hand side of the equations for ${ \dot { a } } , { \dot { e } } .$ , and $\dot { \varpi }$ are in the cosine arguments and that $\varpi$ increases linearly with time at a constant rate $\dot { \varpi }$ determined by secular theory. These equations suggest that $a , \ e .$ , and $\varpi$ will experience sinusoidal variations with maximum amplitudes

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { ( \Delta a ) _ { \mathrm { r e s } } = 2 n \alpha a ( m ^ { \prime } / m _ { \mathrm { c } } ) \left( \left| \displaystyle \frac { C _ { 4 } e } { 2 n ^ { \prime } - n - \dot { \varpi } } \right| + \left| \displaystyle \frac { ( C _ { 5 } - 2 \alpha ) e ^ { \prime } } { 2 n ^ { \prime } - n } \right| \right) , } } \\ { { ( \Delta e ) _ { \mathrm { r e s } } = \left| \displaystyle \frac { n \alpha ( m ^ { \prime } / m _ { \mathrm { c } } ) C _ { 4 } } { 2 n ^ { \prime } - n - \dot { \varpi } } \right| , } } \\ { { ( \Delta \varpi ) _ { \mathrm { r e s } } = \left| \displaystyle \frac { n \alpha ( m ^ { \prime } / m _ { \mathrm { c } } ) ( C _ { 4 } / e ) } { 2 n ^ { \prime } - n - \dot { \varpi } } \right| . } } \end{array}
$$

These are only approximate solutions, particularly in the case of the semi-major axis where we have just combined the amplitudes of the terms associated with each of the two resonant arguments.

Figures $6 . 5 \mathrm { a - d }$ show the result of a full integration of the equations of motion and a comparison with the predicted variations from the combined secular and resonant theory outlined above. The calculations were done with the same starting values as in Sect. 6.9.1, but with $a \ = \ 0 . 6$ in order to place the test particle close to (but not in) the 2:1 jovian resonance. The relevant constants are now $C _ { 1 } = 0 . 3 1 4 0 0 1$ , $C _ { 2 } = - 1 . 2 5 6 0 0$ , $C _ { 3 } = - 0 . 4 4 7 0 0 5$ , $C _ { 4 } = - 1 . 0 4 3 3 2$ , and $C _ { 5 } = 1 . 5 5 2 3 0$ . Note that the magnitudes of $C _ { 1 }$ and $C _ { 2 }$ have increased by a factor of $\sim 2 0$ over those in our secular example with $a = 0 . 1 9 2$ . This is because the separation from Jupiter has decreased, thereby increasing the size of the secular effects. Examination of Fig. 6.5 shows that there is good agreement between the predictions and the numerical results, with the amplitudes and frequencies of the variations in $a , e ,$ , and $\varpi$ being close to their predicted values. We would expect there to be some differences, partly due to our approximate form of Lagrange’s equations and partly due to the fact that in order to integrate the differential equations we took the quantities $a$ and $e$ on the right-hand side of Eqs. (6.181)– (6.183) to be constant, whereas clearly they are varying due to the resonance.

![](images/8fa133452599f0d5a2633693653f813b3e0e48baed1bee1bb51a32c5852d6030.jpg)  
Fig. 6.5. A comparison of the results of a full numerical integration (points or thick line) with predictions from analytical theory (thin line) for the variation of (a) semi-major axis, (b) eccentricity, (c) longitude of perihelion, and (d) longitude of ascending node for a test particle near the 2:1 resonance undergoing resonant and secular perturbations from Jupiter.

Note from Eqs. (6.189)–(6.191) that all the amplitudes contain a divisor of the form $2 n ^ { \prime } - n - \dot { \varpi }$ (i.e., the time derivative of the resonant argument $2 \lambda ^ { \prime } - \lambda - \varpi )$ . This implies that the changes in the elements will become even larger as the exact resonance is approached. However, it is in such circumstances that the assumptions in our simple analytical model break down. We consider a more complete model of resonance in Sect. 8.

# 6.9.3 Short-Period and Small-Amplitude Terms

Knowledge of the form of the disturbing function allows us to isolate the permissible secular arguments and the resonant arguments that are likely to be important. In effect, we are assuming that all other terms involving the mean longitudes are of short period and that their effect will average out to zero; this is the averaging principle. Our comparison of the analytical theory with the full integrations in Figs. 6.4 and 6.5 shows that this is a good approximation. Therefore, although short-period terms exist, their effects appear to be negligible, at least for the examples we chose.

In Sect. 6.9.2 we showed that if we want to know which terms are going to dominate the perturbed motion of the asteroid, we should find those terms for which $j _ { 1 } n ^ { \prime } + j _ { 2 } n \approx 0$ , where $j _ { 1 }$ and $j _ { 2 }$ are integers, because these will contribute to the creation of a small divisor in Eqs. (6.189)–(6.191). Therefore, in the vicinity of 3.27 AU the dominant terms are likely to be those with $j _ { 1 } = \pm 2$ and $j _ { 2 } = \mp 1$ since we will then have $2 n ^ { \prime } - n \approx 0$ . However, this implies that we should also consider the terms with $j _ { 1 } = \pm 4 , \pm 6 , . . .$ and $j _ { 2 } = \mp 2 , \mp 3 , . . .$ since these will also give rise to small divisors. Can there be an infinite number of such terms, all contributing to motion at this resonance?
